questions.question: Under what circumstances MUST a coach break confidentiality?

options.text: Never — confidentiality is absolute in all coaching relationships

Confidentiality must be broken when there is: (1) imminent danger to self (active suicidal ideation with plan/intent), (2) imminent danger to others (credible threat), (3) child/elder abuse (mandated reporting), or (4) court order/subpoena requiring disclosure.
Whenever the coach feels uncomfortable with what the client shared
Only if the client gives written permission to share information

questions.explanation: Confidentiality has clear legal and ethical limits. Coaches MUST break confidentiality for: (1) Imminent danger to self — active suicidal ideation with plan/intent requires connecting to crisis resources. (2) Imminent danger to others — credible threats require warning and reporting. (3) Child/elder abuse — mandated reporting in most jurisdictions. (4) Court order/subpoena — legal requirement. These limits should be communicated upfront in informed consent: 'What we discuss is confidential except if I believe you're in immediate danger, someone else is at risk, or there's abuse of a child or vulnerable adult.'

questions.question: What are 'dual relationships,' and why are they ethically risky in coaching?

options.text: Dual relationships mean coaching two people at once — there's no ethical risk

Dual relationships occur when you have multiple roles with a client (coach + friend, coach + employer, coach + romantic interest). They're risky because: (1) power imbalances are amplified, (2) conflicting interests arise (what's best for coaching may conflict with the other relationship), and (3) exploitation risk increases (vulnerable clients may agree to things they otherwise wouldn't).
Dual relationships are always acceptable as long as both parties agree
Dual relationships only matter for licensed therapists, not coaches

questions.explanation: Dual relationships are when you have multiple roles with the same person: coaching your direct report (coach + boss), dating a current client (coach + romantic partner), coaching a family member (coach + relative), asking a client to invest in your business (coach + business partner). Risks: (1) Power imbalances — coaching inherently has power; adding another role amplifies it. (2) Conflicting interests — what's best for one relationship may harm the other. (3) Exploitation — vulnerable clients may agree out of fear or obligation. Examples to avoid: coaching employees you supervise, dating current/recent clients, coaching family members, financial entanglements with clients.

questions.question: Which of the following is OUTSIDE the scope of practice for a non-clinical coach?

options.text: All of these are outside coaching scope: diagnosing mental health conditions, providing psychotherapy/clinical treatment, prescribing/recommending medications, treating addiction/eating disorders/trauma, and crisis intervention (coaches can recognize and refer, but not intervene).
Only diagnosing mental health conditions is outside scope — everything else is fine
Coaches can do anything therapists can do as long as they don't call it therapy
There are no scope limits for coaches since coaching is unregulated

questions.explanation: Even though coaching is largely unregulated, ethical coaches respect scope limits. Outside coaching scope: (1) Diagnosing mental health conditions (requires clinical license). (2) Providing psychotherapy or clinical treatment (requires clinical training). (3) Prescribing/recommending medications (only MDs/NPs). (4) Treating addiction, eating disorders, trauma (requires specialized clinical training). (5) Crisis intervention — coaches can recognize and refer, not intervene. (6) Legal or financial advice (unless separately licensed). When in doubt, ask: 'Did my training equip me for this? Do I have legal authority? If something goes wrong, would I be liable?' If uncertain, refer to appropriate professional.

questions.question: What are the essential elements of informed consent in a coaching relationship?

options.text: Just verbal agreement is enough — no need for specific elements or documentation

Informed consent should include: (1) nature of coaching vs. therapy ('coaching is not therapy — we focus on goals, not treating conditions'), (2) confidentiality limits, (3) your qualifications ('I'm a certified coach, not a licensed therapist'), (4) structure and fees (session length, cost, cancellation policy), and (5) right to terminate ('you can end coaching anytime'). Document in writing or session notes.
Informed consent only matters for therapists, not coaches
Once consent is given at the start, it never needs to be revisited

questions.explanation: Informed consent ensures the client understands what coaching is and agrees voluntarily. Essential elements: (1) Nature of coaching: 'Coaching is not therapy. We focus on goals, growth, and performance — not treating mental health conditions.' (2) Confidentiality limits: explain when you must break confidentiality. (3) Your qualifications: 'I'm a certified coach through [X], with training in [Y]. I'm not a licensed therapist.' (4) Structure and fees: session length, frequency, cost, cancellation policy. (5) Right to terminate: 'You can end coaching anytime, no questions asked.' Document in writing (email or signed agreement) or in session notes. Informed consent is an ongoing process — revisit if scope changes.

questions.question: A coaching client offers to introduce you to their company's HR director to help you land a corporate coaching contract. What's the ethical response?

options.text: Accept immediately — this is a great business opportunity with no ethical concerns
Decline without explanation to avoid any conflict of interest

Recognize the potential conflict and address it transparently: 'I appreciate that, but I want to make sure our coaching relationship stays focused on your goals, not my business interests. If you genuinely want to introduce me and it won't affect our work, I'm open to it — but I never want you to feel pressured.' Name the ethical consideration and check their true motivation.
End the coaching relationship immediately to avoid the appearance of impropriety

questions.explanation: This scenario presents a potential boundary issue. The client may feel pressured to help you (power imbalance), or you may unconsciously prioritize the client's happiness to secure the contract. Ethical response (opt3): Name the potential conflict explicitly, check the client's true motivation, and make it clear they're under no obligation. This preserves transparency and keeps the client's needs central. Option 1 ignores the ethical consideration. Option 2 may confuse the client without explanation. Option 4 is overly rigid — the relationship can continue if the boundary is named and managed. Key principle: client-first focus with transparent communication about potential conflicts.

title: Ethical Considerations and Professional Standards - Quiz
